exercises.title: REST API Design: Endpoint Table
exercises.description: Print a well-formatted REST API endpoint table for a bookstore. Given a list of resource endpoints with method, path, description, and status code, format them into a readable table with aligned columns. The table should cover: list books (GET), create book (POST), get book (GET /<id>), update book (PATCH /<id>), delete book (DELETE /<id>).
exercises.starterCode:
ENDPOINTS = [
    {"method": "GET",    "path": "/api/v1/books",       "description": "List all books",     "status": 200},
    {"method": "POST",   "path": "/api/v1/books",       "description": "Create a book",      "status": 201},
    {"method": "GET",    "path": "/api/v1/books/<id>",  "description": "Get one book",       "status": 200},
    {"method": "PATCH",  "path": "/api/v1/books/<id>",  "description": "Update a book",      "status": 200},
    {"method": "DELETE", "path": "/api/v1/books/<id>",  "description": "Delete a book",      "status": 204},
]

# TODO: Print a header: "Bookstore REST API"
# TODO: Print a separator line of '=' characters (50 chars wide)
# TODO: Print column headers: METHOD, PATH, DESCRIPTION, STATUS
# TODO: Print each endpoint with consistent column widths:
#   method: 8 chars, path: 25 chars, description: 20 chars, status right-aligned
# TODO: Print a final separator line
exercises.expectedOutput:
Bookstore REST API
==================================================
METHOD   PATH                      DESCRIPTION          STATUS
GET      /api/v1/books             List all books          200
POST     /api/v1/books             Create a book           201
GET      /api/v1/books/<id>        Get one book            200
PATCH    /api/v1/books/<id>        Update a book           200
DELETE   /api/v1/books/<id>        Delete a book           204
==================================================
hints: Use f-string formatting with width specifiers: f"{method:<8}{path:<25}{desc:<20}{status:>6}"
Print the header and separator first, then loop over ENDPOINTS.
'=' * 50 creates a separator of 50 equal signs.

exercises.title: REST API with Consistent Error Handling
exercises.description: Build a complete in-memory REST API simulation. Implement `api_request(method, path, body=None)` that handles GET /tasks, GET /tasks/<id>, POST /tasks, PATCH /tasks/<id>, DELETE /tasks/<id>. Return dicts with 'status', 'body'. Always return consistent error format: `{'error': '...', 'code': '...'}`. Run a sequence of API calls and print status and body.
exercises.starterCode:
tasks_db = {}
next_id = [1]  # Use list to allow mutation in nested scope

def api_request(method, path, body=None):
    """
    Simulate a REST API request.
    Returns {'status': int, 'body': dict}
    """
    parts = path.strip("/").split("/")
    # parts[0] = 'tasks', parts[1] = id (if present)

    # TODO: Handle GET /tasks - return all tasks as list
    # TODO: Handle GET /tasks/<id> - return one task or 404
    # TODO: Handle POST /tasks - validate 'title' in body, create task
    # TODO: Handle PATCH /tasks/<id> - update task fields
    # TODO: Handle DELETE /tasks/<id> - remove task, return 204
    # TODO: Return {'error': 'Method not allowed', 'code': 'METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED'} for unknown methods
    pass

# Test sequence
def print_response(method, path, response):
    print(f"{method} {path} -> {response['status']}")
    import json
    print(json.dumps(response['body'], indent=2))
    print()

r = api_request("POST", "/tasks", {"title": "Buy milk", "priority": "low"})
print_response("POST", "/tasks", r)

r = api_request("POST", "/tasks", {"title": "Write tests"})
print_response("POST", "/tasks", r)

r = api_request("GET", "/tasks")
print_response("GET", "/tasks", r)

r = api_request("PATCH", "/tasks/1", {"priority": "high"})
print_response("PATCH", "/tasks/1", r)

r = api_request("GET", "/tasks/99")
print_response("GET", "/tasks/99", r)

r = api_request("DELETE", "/tasks/2")
print_response("DELETE", "/tasks/2", r)
hints: Parse the path: parts = path.strip('/').split('/') gives ['tasks'] or ['tasks', '1'].
len(parts) == 1 means collection endpoint; len(parts) == 2 means item endpoint.
For POST, default priority to 'medium' if not in body, status to 'pending'.
Use next_id[0] to get and increment the ID counter: task_id = next_id[0]; next_id[0] += 1.

exercises.title: Paginated API Response
exercises.description: Implement pagination for a list API. Write `paginate(items, page, per_page)` that returns a dict with `items`, `total`, `page`, `per_page`, `pages`, `has_next`, `has_prev`. Test with a list of 25 products, showing page 1 (10 per page) and page 3 (10 per page).
exercises.starterCode:
def paginate(items, page, per_page=10):
    """
    Paginate a list of items.
    Returns a dict with pagination metadata.
    """
    # TODO: Calculate total count, total pages
    # TODO: Slice items for the current page
    # TODO: Return dict with: items (slice), total, page, per_page, pages, has_next, has_prev
    pass

# Create 25 sample products
products = [{"id": i, "name": f"Product {i}", "price": i * 9.99} for i in range(1, 26)]

# Page 1, 10 per page
result = paginate(products, page=1, per_page=10)
print(f"Page {result['page']} of {result['pages']}")
print(f"Total items: {result['total']}")
print(f"Showing: {len(result['items'])} items")
print(f"Has next: {result['has_next']} | Has prev: {result['has_prev']}")
print("Items:", [p['name'] for p in result['items']])
print()

# Page 3, 10 per page (last page - only 5 items)
result = paginate(products, page=3, per_page=10)
print(f"Page {result['page']} of {result['pages']}")
print(f"Showing: {len(result['items'])} items")
print(f"Has next: {result['has_next']} | Has prev: {result['has_prev']}")
print("Items:", [p['name'] for p in result['items']])
exercises.expectedOutput:
Page 1 of 3
Total items: 25
Showing: 10 items
Has next: True | Has prev: False
Items: ['Product 1', 'Product 2', 'Product 3', 'Product 4', 'Product 5', 'Product 6', 'Product 7', 'Product 8', 'Product 9', 'Product 10']

Page 3 of 3
Showing: 5 items
Has next: False | Has prev: True
Items: ['Product 21', 'Product 22', 'Product 23', 'Product 24', 'Product 25']
hints: start = (page - 1) * per_page; end = start + per_page; slice = items[start:end]
Total items is just len(items) before slicing.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: List Slicing for Pagination
warmup.starterCode:
items = list(range(1, 11))  # [1..10]
page, per_page = 2, 3
start = (page - 1) * per_page
end = start + per_page
print(items[start:end])
print(f"Page {page}: items {start+1}-{min(end, len(items))} of {len(items)}")
